title: What do you want to be when you grow up?
subtitle: An alternative to this question

blocks.content: He found himself in the neighbourhood of the asteroids 325, 326, 327, 328, 329, and 330. He began, therefore, by visiting them, in order to add to his knowledge.
*Context: It was the summer break after grade 11. I was in Ontario touring universities.*
“I went on a tour of Waterloo and Laurier today, and although I could write in depth about all the facilities I toured, there was one event of the day that I knew instantly I wanted to write about.

To provide some context, we had been looking for a place to eat dinner at our hotel. We were told that there were two restaurants, one on the rooftop that served sushi, and one on the second floor called Rose. Given that it was pretty late already, the reception lady said our best bet was the rooftop restaurant, as it closed later on into the night, while Rose was said to close soon at 10pm.

Going to the rooftop restaurant, we talked to a nice lady, who informed us that, unfortunately, given that we didn’t book a reservation beforehand, there weren’t any spots available at the moment. However, she said that Rose might still be open. Not only that, she helped us call the manager of Rose and reserved us a spot, telling us to just go down there right now, and they would be ready for us. We went down, went into Rose, and were greeted by a very polite gentleman who then seated us at a table that was relatively close to the kitchen.

Although I had some doubts in the beginning (given that we were told Rose was closing at 10), those thoughts barely passed through my mind once we were seated. For the next roughly 15 minutes, our dining experience went on as usual. I chose what I wanted to eat, and then ordered.
I can almost pinpoint the exact moment when I felt my first sense of unease.

After we had ordered, I overheard bits and pieces of a conversation shared by one of the waiters and the chef. Initially, the substance of the conversation wasn’t that clear to me, but from the tone, I sensed the chef was angry at something. It was a mix of very clear passive-aggressiveness, and at times it had descended into rage.

Being someone especially perceptive to this stuff, I immediately became keenly aware of the situation. After eavesdropping for a while, I learned that even though the lady and the manager of Rose agreed to let us eat, apparently the chef was unhappy since he had thought they had already done their last call prior to us coming. Even though this seemed to be only a very slight hiccup, I could tell the chef was furious. He mentioned multiple times that he was just done and wanted to go home, speaking in hushed tones with the manager, and at times seemingly berating his sous chef, a woman who also looked visibly tired.

As they cooked and periodically passive-aggressively argued, I had a growing anger towards the enraged chef. I knew that we were not customers he expected or wanted, and I understood that, but what angered me a lot was the way he treated his sous chef. She obviously had to also stay longer, too, but instead of complaining herself, she kept on cooking, taking on whatever insulting or mean things the enraged chef was saying to her.

As I began fuming with my own anger now, with a jolt, I realized how much I actually related to the enraged chef. Driven by the sheer amount of work he had (and the fact that he still had to somehow get home after 10 and now slightly later), he just couldn’t handle the stress anymore. He likely did this job for a while now, and given how empty this restaurant was compared to the overfilled restaurant on the rooftop, it was clear that the amount of work and hours he put in was rarely recognized. Here he was, stuck day in and day out, working to the bone, being thrown customers that initially wanted to go to the rooftop restaurant but couldn’t, even after the designated closing time… I mean, who wouldn’t be mad in this situation?

As we walked back to our hotel after that dinner, I was mulling over my realization, and I began to reach an even bigger realization: in many ways, my insecurities were reflected in all these individuals.
Much like the rooftop lady, I would at times unfairly push burdens onto others. While not always fully on purpose, the motivation for my intent was clear.
Like the manager of Rose, I said yes to everyone (or at least tried as much as possible to), even if saying no was the better option. These affirmations in turn hurt myself a lot.
Similarly to the berated sous chef, I at times didn’t stand up for myself when I really should have, letting the insults land even when I in no way deserved them.

And of course, I was also the enraged chef, letting all those insecurities get bottled up, and taking my anger out on the people that I often cared about the most, and who I know deserved it the least.”
**The present**
Adults often ask what you want to be when you grow up. And as children, we become so fixated in answering that question. But rarely do we ask ourselves who we want to be when we grow up.
To quote a part of my UBC application essay:
“I want to be the SHAD camp counsellor who made a poster for my dorm room door, decorated with images related to the things I had written about in my application.
I want to be the Ikea employee, who not only agreed but helped me set up a spot to film in Ikea for a school project, moving furniture around so other customers wouldn’t disturb the filming process.

I want to be the German tour guide we had on our Europe trip, who had asked the flight attendants to play “Guten Morgen Sonnenschein” as we flew back to Canada, a song he played for us every morning when we were on the bus travelling to our various destinations.
Because while people might remember what you are, they feel who you are.”
